coredata.dc:title: Protein stabilized fluorescent gold nanocubes as selective probe for alkaline phosphatase via inner filter effect
coredata.dc:creator: Sharma, Amit Kumar
Pandey, Sunil
Khan, M. Shahnawaz
Wu, Hui-Fen
coredata.dc:description: We present a novel approach for the synthesis of unique template free protein stabilized gold nanocubes (PSGNCs) for sensing and quantification of alkaline phosphatase (ALP) in biological fluids. The unique wavelength independent excitation of PSGNCs and absorption of the final product of ALP catalysis, i.e., p-Nitrophenol (p-NP) at 405 nm was exploited for the inner filter effect (IFE) as a sensitive and efficient method to detect ALP. The limit of detection in aqueous solution and serum was found to be 1.616 U/L and 9.441 U/L, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Alkaline phosphatase
Protein stabilized
Gold nanocubes
PSGNC
P-Nitrophenol
Inner filter effect

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: PSGNCs sensitivity of ALP in serum spiked samples
Selectivity against other proteins abundant in serum

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Ministry of Science and Technology of Taiwan
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science and Technology, Taiwan

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We thank the Ministry of Science and Technology of Taiwan (MOST) for financial support with the grant number of MOST 104-2113-M-110-003-MY3 .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of working principles for sensing alkaline phosphatase in the presence of p-Nitrophenyl phosphate (p-NPP). Panel A: ALP catalyzes the conversion of the substrate p-NPP to p-NP in the presence of water in the reaction mixture and with the release of inorganic phosphate (iP). Panel B: As detailed, p-NP absorbs at 405 nm which is also the characteristic absorbance of the newly synthesized PSGNCs. In the absence of ALP, PSGNCs retain their fluorescence at 468 nm. Panel C: In the presence of ALP, the incident 405 nm is absorbed competitively by p-NP, and hence fluorescence of PSGNCs is filtered thereby resulting in reduced emission at 468 nm.

Physical and Optical characteristics of PSGNC (A) UV–vis absorbance spectroscopy of individual components involved in the synthesis of PSGNC; Inset: TEM representative of PSGNC; (B) Fluorescence spectroscopy of PSGNC observed at an excitation wavelength of 370 nm.

(A) Fluorescence intensity of PSGNCs Control (black) and in the presence of varying concentrations ALP preincubated with 1 mM p-Nitrophenyl phosphate (p-NPP); (B) The relative fluorescence intensity was calculated as the ratio of F0/F and plotted against the concentration of ALP.

(A) Fluorescence intensity of PSGNCs Control (black) and in the presence of different concentration of ALP preincubated with 1 mM p-NPP and serum spiked in the reaction volumes. (B) The relative fluorescence intensity calculated as F0/F is shown suggesting the firm sensitivity for different concentrations of ALP.

Identifying selectivity of the sensing system in presence of different serum proteins; ALP (0.1U/ml); Human serum albumin (HSA) and bovine serum albumin (BSA) (25 mg/ml); hemoglobin (Hb) (0.5 μg/ml); thrombin (Th) (1U/ml) and lysozyme (Lys) (0.02U/ml). The concentrations used represent individual protein serum levels.
Comparison of the limit of detection of fluorescence-based detection of alkaline phosphatase.

row.entry: Graphene oxide integrated with λ exonuclease
Carbon dots and MnO2 nano sheets

head.ce:title: Protein stabilized fluorescent gold nanocubes as selective probe for alkaline phosphatase via inner filter effect

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Medical Science and Technology, National Sun Yat-Sen University, Kaohsiung, 80424, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Medical Science and Technology

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry, National Sun Yat-Sen University, Kaohsiung, 70, Lien-Hai Road, Kaohsiung, 80424, Taiwan.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Authors have an equal contribution as the first author.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We present a novel approach for the synthesis of unique template free protein stabilized gold nanocubes (PSGNCs) for sensing and quantification of alkaline phosphatase (ALP) in biological fluids. The unique wavelength independent excitation of PSGNCs and absorption of the final product of ALP catalysis, i.e., p-Nitrophenol (p-NP) at 405 nm was exploited for the inner filter effect (IFE) as a sensitive and efficient method to detect ALP. The limit of detection in aqueous solution and serum was found to be 1.616 U/L and 9.441 U/L, respectively.

ce:section.ce:para: Despite several cardinal instigations for detection of serum alkaline phosphatase (ALP) [1–3], scientists have continually shown great attention to discover novel materials for excellent sensing capability. ALP is a metalloenzyme containing two Zn2+ and one Mg2+ in the active sites, which is found abundantly in the human blood [4] ranging from 25 to 100 U/L. However, increase and decrease in the serum concentration of ALP is associated with many diseases such as liver cirrhosis [5], hepatitis [6], bone disease [7,8] and cancer [9]. Therefore, it is essential to develop a sensitive and selective detection method for ALP, akin to many key enzyme biomarkers such as proteinase K, creatine kinases, glucose oxidase, etc. There are some fundamental challenges in the solution based detection of ALP such as instability of the enzymes, stringent parameters for enzyme activity as well as low detection limits. With the understanding of heroic roles of nanoparticles as sensitive probes for enzyme assays, many protocols have been reported using gold nanoparticles [10,11], silver nanoparticles [12,13], carbon dots [14,15], gold nanoclusters [16,17], modified electrodes [18,19], copper nanoparticles [20] and smart polymers [21], two dimensional transition metal oxides [22] etc. Among the various strategies, colorimetric detections of ALP based on aggregation of nanoparticles such as gold and silver under the influence of ATP phosphorylating/dephosphorylating reaction [12,23] have also been reported. However, the absorbance based colorimetric assays have a limitation of cross-reactivity with similar kind of analytes and hence the sensitivity and stability of such systems become unreliable. The inner filter effect (IFE), in contrast, is a powerful fluorescence-based strategy for detection ALP assay [3,23]. Gabor and Walt first reported IFE in 1991 [24] involving the participation of two chemical moieties where absorption of one species overlaps with the absorption of the other [25,26]. Other mechanisms based on the similar concept of fluorescence quenching are Forster (Fluorescence) Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET), and Photoinduced Electron Transfer (PET) [27–30] applied widely for sensing applications. Relatively, IFE is found to be superior to both FRET and PET as it is easily tunable with enhanced sensitivity of fluorescence signals. The associated simplicity and accuracy is a widely used method for detection of various types of analytes [31–34].

ALP catalyzes the conversion of its substrate p-Nitrophenyl phosphate (p-NPP), a colorless solution, to yellow colored) with absorbance λmax = 405 nm [35]. Existing strategies for sensing ALP have employed this property widely as the basis for detection. Exploring over these attributes, we have used a unique fluorescence property of Protein Stabilized Gold Nano Cubes (PSGNCs) and specific absorption of p-NP for sensitive detection of ALP in a biological fluid with high sensitivity and selectivity.

The PSGNCs have been synthesized via a novel method which is free of any amino acid template and incubating the mixture of gold salt (HAuCl4) and bovine serum albumin (BSA) for 12 h. (The experimental details are provided in Supplementary Material). In an earlier report, Ding et al. have shown the successful synthesis of Protein-Gold Hybrid Nano Cubes in the presence of tryptophan as a template with λmax = 480 nm [36]. The fluorescence characteristic of PSGNCs is applied here by the absorbance of p-NP to act as an inner filter for PSGNCs emission. The intensity of absorption of p-NP at λmax = 405 nm, is proportional to the amount of ALP present in the reaction volume. In the presence of ALP, the absorption of emitted photons by the p-NP results in reduced fluorescence intensity of PSGNCs. Thus, this proves the IFE in the assay of ALP. In contrast to the earlier work [3], our method is highly sensitive to detect 1.616 U/L of ALP and 9.441 U/L in human serum with good reproducibility (S/N = 3). The current approach also opens new avenues for development of nanosensors for many other phosphatases.

To assess the behavior of the detection system under different physical and physiological conditions, it was subjected to varying concentrations of saline, incubated under the influence of different metals, and exposed to a varied range of pH. It was observed that the reaction system was stable nearly at all concentrations of saline prominently at 0.05 M (Fig. S1) while the reaction system is found to be stable at pH 10. (Fig. S2) relating to the alkaline nature of ALP. The presence of Zn2+ resulted in reduced fluorescence intensity as compared to other metals (Fig. S3). This may be due to the inherent affinity of p-NPP towards Zn2+ ions which is also present in ALP.

IFE is a label-free technique which is efficient in detecting smaller concentrations of analytes. p-NP has strong absorption at λ max = 405 nm overlapping with PSGNCs. Owing to this, in the presence of ALP, p-NP competes with the excitation efficiency of PSGNCs and hence the gradual attenuation of emission intensity. Scheme 1 shows the process graphically. Initially, different concentrations of ALP ranging from 1 U, 0.5 U, 0.25 U, 0.125 U, 0.0625 U, 0.312 U were prepared by dilution and incubated with p-NPP in a reaction volume for 30 min at 37 °C. Fig. 2 A shows the fluorescence response after addition of PSGNCs to the reaction volume. The emergence of two emission peaks is attributable to different shapes of nanomaterials in the solution. The emission peak at 468 nm is related to the PSGNCs. It was observed that the sample with a high enzyme concentration of 1 U has least fluorescence intensity while as the concentration reduced to 0.5 U, 0.25 U, 0.125 U and finally 0.0625 U, there was a gradual increase in the fluorescence intensity. The result is explicable in terms of large extinction coefficient 17500 M−1 cm−1 of p-NP. The product p-NP absorbs the entire incident light masking PSGNCs hence resulting in reduced emission at λem = 468 nm. The sequential change in the enzyme concentration occurred proportionally for the formation of p-NP. A higher level of enzyme resulted in large amounts of p-NP while lower levels yielded less amount of p-NP. The introduction of PSGNCs into the solution after the incubation period thereby resulted in a relative change in its fluorescence intensity. Fig. 2B shows a plot of relative fluorescence F0 (initial)/F (final) against the concentration of enzyme and the linear regression was estimated to be R2 = 0.9873 suggesting a linear response. The Limit of Detection (LOD) was found to be 1.616 U/L of the reaction volume (S/N = 3) which corresponds to its sensitivity against the standards of ALP in the human body.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PSGNCs sensitivity of ALP in serum spiked samples
ce:section.ce:para: Inspired by the results obtained with different concentrations of ALP, we further sensed the presence of an enzyme in a biological fluid (serum). Serum was isolated from the blood donated by volunteers in our lab and stored at 4 °C for later use in further experiments. The isolated serum was spiked on similar sets of reaction volume. The samples were again incubated for 30 min at 37 °C. After the incubation period, PSGNCs were introduced to the solution, and the corresponding fluorescence spectra were recorded at an excitation wavelength (λex = 405 nm) as shown in Fig. 3 A. We found that similar trend was repeated in all serum spiked samples. At higher enzyme concentrations, the fluorescence intensity was lower than that at low enzyme concentrations (with high intensity). The relative fluorescence (F0/F) was plotted against the concentration of enzyme, and linear regression was calculated to be R2 = 0.9808 as shown in Fig. 3B. The LOD was found to be 9.441 U/L of the reaction volume.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity against other proteins abundant in serum
ce:section.ce:para: It is known that serum contains a wide variety of proteins and in high abundance, the selectivity of the sensing system in the presence of these proteins was mandatory. Hence, various proteins that may have potential interferences to the activity of ALP for conversion of p-NPP to p-NP have been studied. The choice of proteins was based on their tendency to react with p-NPP. Hence metalloenzymes such as alkaline phosphatase, thrombin, hemoglobin, lysozyme were selected while serum albumin (bovine (BSA) and human (HSA)) were taken due to their abundance in serum, each at their normal serum level concentrations. The relative fluorescence (F0/F) observed in the study shows least fluorescence intensity in the presence of ALP due to the formation of p-NP. In contrast to this, other proteins are unable to convert the substrate p-NPP to p-NP hence no significant reduction in fluorescence intensity was observed (Fig. 4 ).

PSGNCs have been successfully formed via a template-free method using BSA as stabilizers. Due to the unique optical property, the Au material has the capability of sensing the critical biomarker (alkaline phosphatase) with high selectivity and sensitivity. The modus operandi of detection were attributed to the inner filter effect due to PSGNCS and p-NP. Due to high stability under physiological pH and ionic strength, the nanomaterial is ideal for other types of sensors based on papers and electrochemical sensors.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank the Ministry of Science and Technology of Taiwan (MOST) for financial support with the grant number of MOST 104-2113-M-110-003-MY3.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.11.190.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following isSupplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Amit Kumar Sharma completed his B.E. Biotechnology from University of Mumbai (2013) and M.Tech. Biotechnology from D.Y. Patil University (2015). After working under supervision of Prof. Rohit Srivastava, Department of Bioscience and Bioengineering, Indian Institute of Technology Bombay, Mumbai, he joined the Department of Chemistry in NSYSU as PhD Scholar. He has profound interest in synthesis of nanomaterials and exploiting their tunable properties for biomedical applications. He is currently working on development of biomedical sensors as rapid detection tool for various diseases such as cancer and cardiac arrest. He is also synthesizing nanomaterial for rapid and early detection of neurological disorders such as Alzheimer’s Disease.

Dr. Sunil Pandey received his B.S. in 2001 and M.S. in 2003 from University of Mumbai. He later worked under guidance of Prof. Maheshwar Sharon and Prof. Madhuri Sharon to receive his PhD in 2013 from University of Mumbai investigating various synthesis methods of carbon nanomaterials for application in drug delivery. He has explored several different nanomaterials for cancer therapy and detection of associated biomarkers. He is currently working as Post-Doctoral Fellow in Department of Chemistry, NSYSU envisioned to develop handheld cheap and effective rapid sensors for various bioanalytes. He is also determined for effective treatment of cancer by sustained drug delivery and photothermal therapy using two dimensional nanomaterials.

M. Shahnawaz Khan is currently working as Post-Doc Fellow in Department of Chemistry, NSYSU. He did graduate from the same university in Jan 2016. And undergraduate in Honors Industrial Chemistry and Masters in Industrial Chemistry from India’s prestigious university Aligarh M University, INDIA (2009). He is working on targeted drug delivery for curing cancer and employing photo-thermal method for releasing the drug at tumor region.

Dr. Hui-Fen Wu Prof. Hui-Fen Wu currently is a Distinguished Professor in National Sun Yat-Sen University. She received her Ph.D. degree from the University of Texas at Austin, USA in 1994. Followed by postdoctoral appointments at University of California at Berkeley, she went back to Taiwan as an Associate Professor (1996–2001) and Professor (2001–2006) in the Department of Chemistry, Tamkang University. She joined the Department of Chemistry, National Sun Yat-Sen University in 2006 as a senior Professor. Her research work has been focused on Nano-Biomedicine using nanomaterials coupled with Mass Spectrometric techniques, Raman and fluorescence biosensors. Basically, as an analytical chemist, she has diversified her research interests to the application of nano-based biological tools (mass spectrometry, Raman, fluorescence spectroscopy) in bacteriology, biotechnology, oncology, biosensors and drug delivery in cancer and neuro disease treatment. In National Sun Yat-Sen University, she is also an adjunct professor in the Doctoral Degree Program in Marine Biotechnology (2009∼), Center for Nanoscience and Nanotechnology (2008∼2017), Institute of Medical Science and Technology (2011∼). She is also an adjunct professor in the School of Pharmacy, Kaohsiung Medical University (2013∼). She has been served as the editorial board member in more than 20 journals and also serves as an associate editor in Journal of Integrated OMICS. She also served as the reviewer committee for the MOST expensive instrument center, and FDA of Taiwan, a director in Taiwan Society of Mass Spectrometry and Taiwan Society of Proteomics. The awards that she has received included the Lifetime Achievement Award of Marquis Who’s Who in the World (2017), the Academic Outstanding Award (2016) and Outstanding Paper Award (2014) from Taiwan Chemical Sensor Society; the pride of the Nation Inventor & Academic Award (2012); Annual Meeting Contribution Award of the Asia Pacific Society for Materials Research (APSMR) (2017) and APSMR Outstanding Kynote Presentation (2017). To date, she has published 222 scientific journals, 17 book chapters, 7 patents and 256 conference presentation & speeches.
